root_text.an4.122:1.4: Imāni kho, bhikkhave, cattāri bhayāni udakorohantassa pāṭikaṅkhitabbāni.
root_text.an4.122:1.5: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, cattāri bhayāni idhekaccassa kulaputtassa imasmiṁ dhammavinaye agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajitassa pāṭikaṅkhitabbāni.
root_text.an4.122:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco kulaputto saddhā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito hoti:
root_text.an4.122:2.3: ‘otiṇṇomhi jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, dukkhotiṇṇo dukkhapareto;
root_text.an4.122:2.4: appeva nāma imassa kevalassa dukkhakkhandhassa antakiriyā paññāyethā’ti.
root_text.an4.122:2.5: Tamenaṁ tathā pabbajitaṁ samānaṁ sabrahmacārino ovadanti anusāsanti:
root_text.an4.122:2.6: ‘evaṁ te abhikkamitabbaṁ, evaṁ te paṭikkamitabbaṁ, evaṁ te āloketabbaṁ, evaṁ te viloketabbaṁ, evaṁ te samiñjitabbaṁ, evaṁ te pasāritabbaṁ, evaṁ te saṅghāṭipattacīvaraṁ dhāretabban’ti.
root_text.an4.122:2.8: ‘mayaṁ kho pubbe agāriyabhūtā samānā aññe ovadāmapi anusāsāmapi.
root_text.an4.122:2.9: Ime panamhākaṁ puttamattā maññe nattamattā maññe ovaditabbaṁ anusāsitabbaṁ maññantī’ti.
root_text.an4.122:2.10: So kupito anattamano sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvattati.
root_text.an4.122:2.11: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ūmibhayassa bhīto sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvatto.
root_text.an4.122:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco kulaputto saddhā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito hoti:
root_text.an4.122:3.3: ‘otiṇṇomhi jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, dukkhotiṇṇo dukkhapareto;
root_text.an4.122:3.4: appeva nāma imassa kevalassa dukkhakkhandhassa antakiriyā paññāyethā’ti.
root_text.an4.122:3.5: Tamenaṁ tathā pabbajitaṁ samānaṁ sabrahmacārino ovadanti anusāsanti:
root_text.an4.122:3.6: ‘idaṁ te khāditabbaṁ, idaṁ te na khāditabbaṁ, idaṁ te bhuñjitabbaṁ, idaṁ te na bhuñjitabbaṁ, idaṁ te sāyitabbaṁ, idaṁ te na sāyitabbaṁ, idaṁ te pātabbaṁ, idaṁ te na pātabbaṁ, kappiyaṁ te khāditabbaṁ, akappiyaṁ te na khāditabbaṁ, kappiyaṁ te bhuñjitabbaṁ, akappiyaṁ te na bhuñjitabbaṁ, kappiyaṁ te sāyitabbaṁ, akappiyaṁ te na sāyitabbaṁ, kappiyaṁ te pātabbaṁ, akappiyaṁ te na pātabbaṁ, kāle te khāditabbaṁ, vikāle te na khāditabbaṁ, kāle te bhuñjitabbaṁ, vikāle te na bhuñjitabbaṁ, kāle te sāyitabbaṁ, vikāle te na sāyitabbaṁ, kāle te pātabbaṁ, vikāle te na pātabban’ti.
root_text.an4.122:3.8: ‘mayaṁ kho pubbe agāriyabhūtā samānā yaṁ icchāma taṁ khādāma, yaṁ na icchāma na taṁ khādāma;
root_text.an4.122:3.9: yaṁ icchāma taṁ bhuñjāma, yaṁ na icchāma na taṁ bhuñjāma;
root_text.an4.122:3.10: yaṁ icchāma taṁ sāyāma, yaṁ na icchāma na taṁ sāyāma;
root_text.an4.122:3.11: yaṁ icchāma taṁ pivāma, yaṁ na icchāma na taṁ pivāma;
root_text.an4.122:3.12: kappiyampi khādāma akappiyampi khādāma kappiyampi bhuñjāma akappiyampi bhuñjāma kappiyampi sāyāma akappiyampi sāyāma kappiyampi pivāma akappiyampi pivāma, kālepi khādāma vikālepi khādāma kālepi bhuñjāma vikālepi bhuñjāma kālepi sāyāma vikālepi sāyāma kālepi pivāma vikālepi pivāma;
root_text.an4.122:3.13: yampi no saddhā gahapatikā divā vikāle paṇītaṁ khādanīyaṁ vā bhojanīyaṁ vā denti, tatrapime mukhāvaraṇaṁ maññe karontī’ti.
root_text.an4.122:3.14: So kupito anattamano sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvattati.
root_text.an4.122:3.15: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kumbhīlabhayassa bhīto sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvatto.
root_text.an4.122:4.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco kulaputto saddhā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito hoti:
root_text.an4.122:4.3: ‘otiṇṇomhi jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi, dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi dukkhotiṇṇo dukkhapareto;
root_text.an4.122:4.4: appeva nāma imassa kevalassa dukkhakkhandhassa antakiriyā paññāyethā’ti.
root_text.an4.122:4.5: So evaṁ pabbajito samāno pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya gāmaṁ vā nigamaṁ vā piṇḍāya pavisati arakkhiteneva kāyena arakkhitāya vācāya arakkhitena cittena anupaṭṭhitāya satiyā asaṁvutehi indriyehi.
root_text.an4.122:4.6: So tattha passati gahapatiṁ vā gahapatiputtaṁ vā pañcahi kāmaguṇehi samappitaṁ samaṅgībhūtaṁ paricārayamānaṁ.
root_text.an4.122:4.8: ‘mayaṁ kho pubbe agāriyabhūtā samānā pañcahi kāmaguṇehi samappitā samaṅgībhūtā paricārimhā;
root_text.an4.122:4.9: saṁvijjanti kho pana me kule bhogā.
root_text.an4.122:4.10: Sakkā bhoge ca bhuñjituṁ puññāni ca kātuṁ.
root_text.an4.122:4.11: Yannūnāhaṁ sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvattitvā bhoge ca bhuñjeyyaṁ puññāni ca kareyyan’ti.
root_text.an4.122:4.13: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu āvaṭṭabhayassa bhīto sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvatto.
root_text.an4.122:4.14: Āvaṭṭabhayanti kho, bhikkhave, pañcannetaṁ kāmaguṇānaṁ adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.an4.122:5.2: Idha, bhikkhave, ekacco kulaputto saddhā agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajito hoti:
root_text.an4.122:5.3: ‘otiṇṇomhi jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi, dukkhotiṇṇo dukkhapareto;
root_text.an4.122:5.4: appeva nāma imassa kevalassa dukkhakkhandhassa antakiriyā paññāyethā’ti.
root_text.an4.122:5.5: So evaṁ pabbajito samāno pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya gāmaṁ vā nigamaṁ vā piṇḍāya pavisati arakkhiteneva kāyena arakkhitāya vācāya arakkhitena cittena anupaṭṭhitāya satiyā asaṁvutehi indriyehi.
root_text.an4.122:5.6: So tattha passati mātugāmaṁ dunnivatthaṁ vā duppārutaṁ vā.
root_text.an4.122:5.7: Tassa mātugāmaṁ disvā dunnivatthaṁ vā duppārutaṁ vā rāgo cittaṁ anuddhaṁseti.
root_text.an4.122:5.8: So rāgānuddhaṁsitena cittena sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvattati.
root_text.an4.122:5.9: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu susukābhayassa bhīto sikkhaṁ paccakkhāya hīnāyāvatto.
root_text.an4.122:5.12: Imāni kho, bhikkhave, cattāri bhayāni idhekaccassa kulaputtassa imasmiṁ dhammavinaye agārasmā anagāriyaṁ pabbajitassa pāṭikaṅkhitabbānī”ti.

translation_text.an4.122:1.1: “Mendicants, anyone who enters the water should anticipate four dangers.
translation_text.an4.122:1.3: The dangers of waves, gharials, whirlpools, and sharks.
translation_text.an4.122:1.4: These are the four dangers that anyone who enters the water should anticipate.
translation_text.an4.122:1.5: In the same way, a gentleman gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in this teaching and training should anticipate four dangers.
translation_text.an4.122:1.7: The dangers of waves, gharials, whirlpools, and sharks.
translation_text.an4.122:2.1: And what, mendicants, is the danger of waves?
translation_text.an4.122:2.2: It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness, thinking:
translation_text.an4.122:2.3: ‘I’m swamped by rebirth, old age, and death; by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. I’m swamped by suffering, mired in suffering.
translation_text.an4.122:2.4: Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.’
translation_text.an4.122:2.5: When they’ve gone forth, their spiritual companions advise and instruct them:
translation_text.an4.122:2.6: ‘You should go out like this, and come back like that. You should look to the front like this, and to the side like that. You should contract your limbs like this, and extend them like that. This is how you should bear your outer robe, bowl, and robes.’
translation_text.an4.122:2.8: ‘Formerly, as laypeople, we advised and instructed others.
translation_text.an4.122:2.9: And now these mendicants—who you’d think were our children or grandchildren—imagine they can advise and instruct us!’
translation_text.an4.122:2.10: Angry and upset, they resign the training and return to a lesser life.
translation_text.an4.122:2.11: This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life for fear of the danger of waves.
translation_text.an4.122:2.12: ‘Danger of waves’ is a term for anger and distress.
translation_text.an4.122:2.13: This is called the danger of waves.
translation_text.an4.122:3.1: And what, mendicants, is the danger of gharials?
translation_text.an4.122:3.2: It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness …
translation_text.an4.122:3.5: When they’ve gone forth, their spiritual companions advise and instruct them:
translation_text.an4.122:3.6: ‘You may eat, consume, taste, and drink these things, but not those. You may eat what’s allowable, but not what’s unallowable. You may eat at the right time, but not at the wrong time.’
translation_text.an4.122:3.8: ‘Formerly, as laypeople, we used to eat, consume, taste, and drink what we wanted, not what we didn’t want.
translation_text.an4.122:3.12: We ate and drank both allowable and unallowable things, at the right time and the wrong time.
translation_text.an4.122:3.13: And these faithful householders give us delicious fresh and cooked foods at the wrong time of day. But these guys imagine they can gag our mouths!’
translation_text.an4.122:3.14: Angry and upset, they resign the training and return to a lesser life.
translation_text.an4.122:3.15: This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life for fear of the danger of gharials.
translation_text.an4.122:3.16: ‘Danger of gharials’ is a term for gluttony.
translation_text.an4.122:3.17: This is called the danger of gharials.
translation_text.an4.122:4.1: And what, mendicants, is the danger of whirlpools?
translation_text.an4.122:4.2: It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness …
translation_text.an4.122:4.5: When they’ve gone forth, they robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties.
translation_text.an4.122:4.6: There they see a householder or their child amusing themselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.an4.122:4.8: ‘Formerly, as laypeople, we amused ourselves, supplied and provided with the five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.an4.122:4.9: And it’s true that my family is wealthy.
translation_text.an4.122:4.10: I can both enjoy my wealth and make merit.
translation_text.an4.122:4.11: Why don’t I resign the training and return to a lesser life, so I can enjoy my wealth and make merit?’
translation_text.an4.122:4.12: They resign the training and return to a lesser life.
translation_text.an4.122:4.13: This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life for fear of the danger of whirlpools.
translation_text.an4.122:4.14: ‘Danger of whirlpools’ is a term for the five kinds of sensual stimulation.
translation_text.an4.122:4.15: This is called the danger of whirlpools.
translation_text.an4.122:5.1: And what, mendicants, is the danger of sharks?
translation_text.an4.122:5.2: It’s when a gentleman has gone forth out of faith from the lay life to homelessness …
translation_text.an4.122:5.5: When they’ve gone forth, they robe up in the morning and, taking their bowl and robe, enter a village or town for alms without guarding body, speech, and mind, without establishing mindfulness, and without restraining the sense faculties.
translation_text.an4.122:5.6: There they see a lady scantily clad, with revealing clothes.
translation_text.an4.122:5.8: so they resign the training and return to a lesser life.
translation_text.an4.122:5.9: This is called a mendicant who disavows the training and returns to a lesser life because they’re afraid of the danger of sharks.
translation_text.an4.122:5.10: ‘Danger of sharks’ is a term for ladies.
translation_text.an4.122:5.11: This is called the danger of sharks.
translation_text.an4.122:5.12: These are the four dangers that a gentleman gone forth from the lay life to homelessness in this teaching and training should anticipate.”

variant_text.an4.122:1.5: dhammavinaye agārasmā → saddhā agārasmā (bj, sya-all, km) | pabbajitassa → pabbajato (bj); pabbajito (sya-all); pabbajite (pts1ed)
